---
title: "009 — What connects states in the EV transition? A network horse race"
date: 2026-06-12
---

## Question

We never observe how states influence each other's EV adoption — only the
outcomes. So we make three candidate influence networks compete: **geography**
(shared borders), **economic similarity** (income/urbanization/infra), and an
**inferred co-adoption network** (states whose EV-share changes co-move beyond
autocorrelation noise). Each predicts a held-out state's adoption year from its
neighbours; the best predictor is the best model of the latent structure.
(Background: *Adoption-Network Inference* card in the [guide](guide.qmd).)

## Setup

```{python}
import duckdb
import networkx as nx
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from complexity_lab.config import settings
from complexity_lab.networks.contagion import load_adjacency, observed_adoption_years
from complexity_lab.networks.inference import (
    coadoption_graph, economic_similarity_graph, horserace,
)

con = duckdb.connect(str(settings.db_path), read_only=True)
panel = con.execute(
    "SELECT state_code, year, ev_share, pc_income_inr, urban_pct, ev_chargers, cng_stations "
    "FROM panel_state_year WHERE state_code <> 'ALL'"
).df()
observed = observed_adoption_years(panel, threshold=0.02)

g_geo = load_adjacency(con)
cov = (panel.sort_values("year").groupby("state_code")
       [["pc_income_inr", "urban_pct", "ev_chargers", "cng_stations"]].last())
g_econ = economic_similarity_graph(cov, k=4)
shares = panel.pivot_table(index="year", columns="state_code", values="ev_share")
g_coad = coadoption_graph(shares, alpha=0.05)
print(f"edges — geographic {g_geo.number_of_edges()}, economic {g_econ.number_of_edges()}, "
      f"co-adoption {g_coad.number_of_edges()}")
```

## The race

```{python}
race = horserace({"geographic": g_geo, "economic_similarity": g_econ,
                  "co_adoption": g_coad}, observed)
race.round(2)
```

```{python}
px.bar(race.reset_index(), x="network", y="mae_years", text_auto=".2f",
       title="Leave-one-out error predicting each state's EV adoption year (lower = better)")
```

## Inside the winning network

```{python}
comms = nx.community.louvain_communities(g_coad, seed=42)
rows = [{"state": s, "community": i} for i, c in enumerate(comms) for s in c]
deg = pd.Series(dict(g_coad.degree(weight="weight")), name="strength")
top = deg.sort_values(ascending=False).head(12).reset_index()
top.columns = ["state", "strength"]
px.bar(top, x="strength", y="state", orientation="h",
       title="Most-connected states in the co-adoption network (synchrony hubs)")
```

## Out-of-sample: does the edge survive a temporal split?

The in-sample race could flatter the co-adoption network (it saw the whole
history). Rebuild it from **pre-2022 data only**, then predict the states that
crossed the threshold *after* 2022 from neighbours that crossed before:

```{python}
from complexity_lab.networks.inference import (
    rewiring_null_test, temporal_holdout_horserace,
)
g_coad_pre = coadoption_graph(shares.loc[shares.index <= 2022], alpha=0.05)
oos = temporal_holdout_horserace(
    {"geographic": g_geo, "economic_similarity": g_econ, "co_adoption_pre": g_coad_pre},
    observed, split_year=2022)
oos.round(2)
```

## Is it the wiring, or just the degrees? (rewiring null)

```{python}
null = rewiring_null_test(g_coad, observed, n_rewires=200)
print(f"real-network LOO MAE = {null['observed_mae']:.2f} years vs "
      f"degree-preserving rewired mean = {null['null_mean_mae']:.2f} "
      f"(5th pct {null['null_p5_mae']:.2f}); p = {null['p_value']:.3f} "
      f"over {null['n_null']} rewires")
```

A small p says the *specific edges* — not merely how connected each state is —
carry the predictive signal.

## Finding

```{python}
w = race["mae_years"].idxmin()
print(f"Winner: {w} (MAE {race.loc[w,'mae_years']:.2f} years) vs "
      + ", ".join(f"{n} {race.loc[n,'mae_years']:.2f}" for n in race.index if n != w))
```

The inferred **co-adoption network predicts adoption timing better than
geography or economic similarity**. Read carefully, that is a substantive
result: state EV adoption synchrony is not primarily border spillover, and not
just "rich states move together" — it reflects a national wave structure
(policy timing, OEM launch waves, charging-network corridors) that the
co-movement filter picks up directly. The honest caveats: ~14 yearly
observations limit the edge test's power, and co-adoption models *synchrony*,
not direction of influence. The paper-grade upgrade is the maximum-entropy
(BiCM) null with out-of-sample year prediction — blueprint Project A, where
this notebook is the working skeleton.
